data.title: Tesla: Sell The Rally, Autonomous Robots Do Not Justify Sky-High Valuation
data.description:
Tesla, Inc. (NASDAQ:TSLA) stock rallied by 16% following the release of its Q3 2024 earnings report, buoyed by an improvement in profit margins. While the EV business remains a key revenue and profit driver at present, the main determinant of the stock's valuation hinges on its autonomous robot endeavors.

Tesla wants to prove that it is not just a car company, but a full-fledged technology company, and revealed its latest AI-powered autonomous machines at the "We, Robot" event earlier this month. Much has been said about its underwhelming "Cybercab" announcement and vague timeline, while competitors like Alphabet Inc.'s (GOOG) (GOOGL) Waymo are already commercially monetizing their fleet of autonomous vehicles in select cities. Though, the company also showed off another important product at the event, namely the Optimus Generation 2 humanoid robot, which is what we will be covering in this article.

In the previous article, we delved deeply into Tesla's end-to-end AI strategy, whereby:

"Tesla is shifting away from software codes, and instead just relying on a single, large neural network responsible for all aspects of driving."

CEO Elon Musk has been proclaiming that this same approach can be applied to its humanoid robot development and training.

This new approach had lifted market expectations for speedier advancements. However, the underwhelming demonstrations at the 'We, Robot' event have culminated in a mismatch between real progress and the stock's sky-high valuation, inducing a downgrade of the stock to a "sell" rating.

A reality check on Tesla's robotic endeavors

TSLA currently trades at a Forward P/E multiple of around 100x, making it the most expensive Magnificent 7 stock.

A key element of the bull case has been Tesla's pivot to the end-to-end AI approach, raising expectations of a much faster iteration cycle, and consequently bringing forward the anticipated timeline for commercial launch.

At "We, Robot" on October 10th, Tesla showcased impressive advancements in the dexterity of its humanoids. However, the revelation that the Optimus robots at the event were being teleoperated by humans, rather than the humanoid machines dancing, serving drinks, and interacting with guests on their own, was a disappointment for many investors. It raised questions around Tesla's actual progress on autonomous AI functionalities.

CEO Elon Musk has been making some bold predictions around its Optimus robots. At the "We, Robot" event, Musk suggested that all 8 billion people on the planet will want one. Of course, that could be said for practically any product. Back in June 2024 at a shareholder meeting, Musk said that its humanoid robot business line could drive the company's valuation to $25 trillion one day, without any specific timeframe. The CEO is known for selling a big vision to its shareholders to buoy stock market performance. In his defense, sustaining strong share price performance is key to keeping stock-based compensated employees motivated to continue innovating at Tesla.

Nonetheless, to sustain a lofty valuation of 95x forward earnings, market participants would rightfully expect more details around the commercial viability of the product. These would include such as any improvements in power efficiency, given that electrical consumption costs are one of the key hindrances to robot adoption.

The rapidly developing automation technology can help speed up operations and lift some of the burden off human workers, but the tools have a new set of requirements such as access to far more electrical power and a strong internet signal.

Building that capability can be particularly difficult for industrial operators that are located in rural areas far from existing infrastructure, or those in urban areas where there are heavy demands on the power grid.

- The Wall Street Journal.

Furthermore, at the event, Musk offered a vague target price range over an unspecified period:

I think at scale this would cost something like I don't know $20,000 to $30,000. Probably less than a car is my prediction long term.

Now, stating how much the product could cost in the distant future is of little value for a company that already has a track record of missing their predicted product price targets.

He is talking about scale. What does scale mean? That is the question for him. He has a long history of saying these products will cost 'X' and twice as much when they actually come out.

- Tim Higgins, business columnist at the The Wall Street Journal in an interview with CNBC.

Given Musk's unclear timeframe for achieving the $20,000-$30,000 price range, it is fair to assume that it will likely be a long way off until they are affordable enough to induce mass-scale adoption.

Furthermore, back in June, CEO Elon Musk also shared that he expects the company to begin selling its Optimus robots commercially to other companies by 2026 while extending internal use in Tesla's own factories during 2025.

The company has already highlighted that two Optimus robots have been deployed in Tesla's own factory, enabling the company to test out how these humanoids function in industrial environments and resolve any issues before external commercialization. This offers a notable advantage over competitors in the space that doesn't have their own auto manufacturing (or other industrial) business lines for internal testing and iterations.

Nonetheless, it is worth considering the fact that rivals have been forging partnerships with leading auto manufacturers to test out their humanoids for such manual labor and learn together how best to deploy these robots at a mass scale in the future. For instance, competitor Apptronik has cultivated a partnership with Mercedes-Benz Group AG (OTCPK:MBGAF) and plans a broader commercial launch of its humanoid in 2025. Nvidia-backed Figure AI boasts a similar partnership with Bayerische Motoren Werke Aktiengesellschaft (OTCPK:BMWYY), which has already been delivering some fruitful progress.

During a trial run lasting several weeks at BMW Group Plant Spartanburg, the latest humanoid robots Figure 02 from California company Figure successfully inserted sheet metal parts into specific fixtures, which were then assembled as part of the chassis. The robot must be particularly dexterous to complete this production step.

…

During the trial run, the BMW Group gained valuable knowledge of what requirements must be met in order to be able to integrate so-called multi-purpose robots into an existing production system. This includes how humanoid robots communicate with the system under real conditions. After the first use in automobile production, the experts at the BMW Group and Figure are collaborating to prepare Figure for future applications in production and continuing to develop the robots, based on the findings. - BMW Group.

Given the progress rivals are making in readying for commercial launch, Tesla is unlikely to benefit from a wide moat when Optimus enters the market. It could end up having to engage in a price war to take market share in a crowded space, similar to its current plight in the EV market. Consequently, this puts at risk Tesla's ability to sustain such an expensive valuation multiple of 95x forward earnings.

Counterarguments to the bearish thesis

Now taking the "glass half full" perspective, the outlook for robotics demand is improving, with Goldman Sachs estimating that the total addressable market for humanoids could reach $38 billion by 2035, revised upwards from a $6 billion estimate previously. Hence, the pie to be shared among the various robotics vendors is growing.

Considering current technological capabilities, we view humanoid robots as having visible demand from jobs in structured environment such as manufacturing (e.g., EV assembly and component sorting). Given its likely adaptability to dynamic environment enabled by AI algorithms, we believe humanoids look particularly appealing for special operations such as dangerous and hazardous tasks, considering the associated fatality rate and people's low willingness in doing such jobs thus customers' likely willingness to pay a higher price than typical manufacturing work.

- Goldman Sachs.

Indeed, robot vendors like Tesla could still benefit from strong pricing power. Industrial companies could be willing to pay higher prices for the robots as long as the Return on Investment (ROI) is appealing, in the sense of higher productivity and redundant health insurance (and other employee-related) costs.

Furthermore, the Tesla Optimus robot is better positioned relative to most competitors' products, in the form of possessing more capabilities that can be deployed in industrial environments.

Nevertheless, competition should not be underestimated, as Apptronik's "Apollo" robot still boasts the widest array of targeted applications.

That being said, the presence of more players in the market actually brings advantages to Tesla as well. Unlike when the company pioneered the commercialization of EVs, which incurred Tesla having to do most of the heavy lifting in cultivating the manufacturing process and subsequent supply chains, the robotics market is benefitting from a more concerted effort. This is conducive to speedier supply chain developments. It has resulted in faster declines in the cost of manufacturing robots, relative to the earlier days of the EV market.

We are seeing stronger commitment from the supply chain, start-ups in the US and Asia, multiple listed players setting up new robot divisions and potentially more government support (i.e., from China).

…

We note the manufacturing cost for the humanoid robot has dropped significantly from likely $50-250k per unit last year to $30-150k (range of low spec to high spec) now, or around a 40% decline (vs. our prior assumption of 15% to 20% p.a decline) mainly driven by the availability of cheaper components with a broader scope of supply chain options

- Goldman Sachs.

So while there is skepticism around Tesla's ability to achieve the targeted product price range of $20,000 to $30,000 profitably given Tesla's history, declining component costs certainly offer the hope that Tesla can achieve its targeted business economics in the robotics segment.

Keep in mind also the fact that the company has been witnessing profit margin compression over the past several quarters as it fights a price war in the EV market.

Tesla is investing heavily in NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) GPUs to power its Dojo supercomputer, which in turn powers its autonomous vehicle and Optimus robot endeavors. Such heavy CapEx investments are putting pressure on Tesla's bottom line.

That being said, the company did deliver a bounce in profit margins in Q3 thanks to improvements in cost efficiencies.

The growing total addressable market ("TAM") for robotics and Tesla's competitive positioning that could accentuate future pricing power should be supportive of the company's profitability prospects going forward.

In fact, on the Q3 2024 Tesla earnings call, Elon Musk emphasized the company's scaling prowess over its competitors:

We're moreover the only company that really has all of the ingredients necessary to scale human robots. Because the things that other companies are missing is that they're missing the AI brain, and they're missing the ability to really scale to very high-volume production.

Nonetheless, these claims will be put to the test once the Optimus robots officially hit the market, competing against fiercely competitive Chinese manufacturers as well.

Forget Tesla, just own Nvidia instead

While the Forward P/E ratio is a popular valuation metric, a more comprehensive measure is the Forward Price-Earnings-Growth (Forward PEG) multiple, which essentially adjusts the Forward P/E by the anticipated earnings growth rate going forward.

Out of all the Magnificent 7 stocks, Tesla has one of the lowest projected EPS growth rates at 12.41%.

Now, adjusting each stock's Forward P/E ratio by the anticipated earnings growth rates gives us the following Forward PEG multiples.

Tesla is by far the most expensive Magnificent 7 stock at a staggering Forward PEG multiple of 7.65x. TSLA simply does not deserve to trade at such a lofty premium over other prominent AI winners, most notably Nvidia.

In fact, in a recent article, we delved into Nvidia's strong positioning to capitalize on the robotics revolution. The semiconductor giant is not only benefitting from selling increasingly powerful chips and supercomputers to power these robotic workloads, but is also shrewdly setting itself up for long-term recurring software revenue through the "Omniverse" operating system.

So while robot vendors like Tesla, Apptronik, Figure AI, and Boston Dynamics are yet to commercially launch their products, and will inevitably end up fighting for market share, Nvidia is already profiting. This is given the number of robotics companies that are relying on the company's technology. Over the coming years/ decades, as these vendors continuously iterate their products, both on the hardware and software level, to make them compatible for industrial/consumer use, demand for Nvidia's technology should sustainably grow. This is regardless of which robotics company ultimately comes out on top in the end market.

NVDA also offers a more ethical investment option to ride the autonomous robotics wave, given the reports of fatalities during Tesla's testing of "Full Self-Driving." I, personally, refuse to profit from the shares of a technology company that has been putting people's lives at risk.

On the whole, if you are looking for strong exposure to the robotics secular growth story, you're better off owning NVDA at 1.32x Forward PEG than TSLA at an astounding 7.65x.

Tesla stock has been downgraded to a "sell" rating.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Hilton Worldwide: Hotel Count Growth Is In The Price After A Mixed Q3 (Downgrade)
data.description:
Shares of Hilton Worldwide (NYSE:HLT) have been a strong performer over the past year, gaining 57%; however, shares did decline by over 2% on Wednesday in response to Q3 earnings and disappointing guidance. I last covered HLT in October 2023, rating shares a “buy,” and since then, the stock has gained 54%, 20% ahead of the S&P 500. Given such strong performance and updated financials, now is a good time to revisit Hilton.

In the company’s third quarter, Hilton earned $1.92, beating consensus by $0.08 even as revenue of $2.87 billion was $40 million below consensus. HLT generated $904 million in adjusted EBITDA, up 8.4% from last year, and it expanded margins to a robust 72.2% adjusted EBITDA margin, up 140bps from last year. Because of its comparatively fixed operating costs, G&A expenses rose by a more modest 5% to $101 million.

Hilton generates such tremendous margins because it is really a brand company, franchising out the hotels. Most of the hotels and associated real estate are owned by its franchisees, leaving Hilton as an exceedingly capital-light business. Indeed, it spent just $57 million in cap-ex last quarter. The key thing for Hilton is seeing growing results in its franchised hotels and expanding its franchise base.

Looking at its results, revenue per available room (RevPAR) is the key metric of a hotel’s productivity, and it rose by 1.4% on a constant currency basis to $121.40. Occupancy rose by 30bps to 75.3%. As you can see below, Europe was the standout, up 2.3% to 81.3% while Asia was the laggard, down 50bps to 73.2%.

Europe took a bit longer to recovery from COVID than the US, but it has fully caught up. Asia Pacific has been weighed down by weak results in China. China was down 9% while the rest of Asia was up 4%. It is seeing little international travel into China. Management expects China to be better next year given stimulus. Government spending efforts should help to arrest the deterioration in this critical market, but I am cautious as to the timing and magnitude of the recovery, particularly given ongoing US-China tensions, which seems to have weighed on US brands in the Chinese market.

Interestingly, there has been a bit of divergence among Hilton’s brands. Hilton’s two upscale brands, Waldorf Astoria and Curio, reported over 4% gains in RevPAR, the strongest of any brands. Its lower end Hampton and Homewood Suites were flat over the past year. This argues that higher-end consumers are holding up better than lower-end consumers, which makes sense as the cumulative impact of inflation over the past three years has likely done more to wipe out excess savings of those with lower incomes.

Indeed, as you can see below, after steadily rising post-pandemic, spending on travel has stalled out over the past few quarters as consumers’ discretionary budgets come under pressure. In an acknowledgement of this, Hilton trimmed guidance. It expects RevPAR to be up 2-2.5% this year, down from 2-3% previously. It continues to expect $6.93-$7.03 in EPS. These revisions are driven by a slowing in Q4 with RevPAR up just 1-2%, and adjusted EBITDA declining by at least $70 million sequentially. As such, it expects $1.57-$1.67 in adjusted EPS well below the $1.74 consensus for Q4.

While per-hotel growth is slowing, Hilton has benefited from a significant expansion in its operations. It opened 531 hotels with 36,600 rooms in Q3, a company record. Accordingly, unit growth was 7.8% from last year, a record. This increase in the unit count combined with modest RevPAR growth allowed management and franchise fee revenues to rise by 8.3%.

Hilton now has 8,200 hotels in its system, and after Q3, it now has over 700 hotels in China. This has become a critical market for the company, and until we see China return to growth, overall growth is likely to be constrained.

Management expects growth in 2025 to be similar to 2024. It expects to see Europe and the Middle East slow but remain the best region next year while the pace of decline in China should be smaller given easier comparisons. These factors should broadly offset. Absent a recession (which is not my base case), activity in the US should be somewhat positive. I think it is important to note that even with its rebound, travel spending is a smaller share of consumption than pre-pandemic.

In other words, consumers are not “over-spending” on travel even as it seems like the sector has boomed. Rather, the level is fairly normal with arguably some room for growth. As such, I expect travel spending to be at least as fast as consumption over the next year, though the deceleration in RevPAR trends does point to ongoing divergence by income-level.

The bigger driver of Hilton’s growth is going to be its expansion efforts. It has 492k room in its development pipeline across 3,525 hotels, up 8% from last year. These efforts will grow its hotel count by over 40%. Importantly, its franchisees face most of the cost of this expansion, as evidenced by HLT’s minimal cap-ex needs. This pipeline will take several years to complete, but 48% of its pipeline is under construction.

With ~1,700 hotels under construction, HLT is poised to grow its hotel count by 6+% per year (or at least 500 hotels) over each of the next 3-5 years. I would also note that about 57% of its development pipeline is outside of the US as it continues to expand its footprint and enter higher growth markets where it has less saturation.

With its cash flow from franchise fees, Hilton has been aggressively returning capital to shareholders. It has done $2.4 billion of capital return so far this year and should end 2024 at about $3 billion. This has resulted in a 5% decline in the share count over the past year. Due in part to capital returns, its debt load has risen from $9.2 billion to $11.3 billion in debt. As such, interest expense rose to $140 million from $113 million last year.

Still, its balance sheet is healthy given the recurring nature of its cash flows. It has just 2.8x net debt to EBITDA, and only $500 million in debt matures before 2027. With $1.65 billion in cash, it can pay that maturity out of cash. As such, Hilton does not have much exposure to higher interest rates as rates could be low again by the time it needs to refinance debt.

Hilton’s aggressive hotel count growth should enable solid earnings growth to continue; however, Q3 results do speak to slower growth in its existing business. Still, with ~6% hotel count growth and 0-2% RevPAR growth, HLT should be able to grow revenue by about 7% per year and earnings by 10-12% over 2025-2027. Combined with ~3-5% share count reduction, EPS should rise by about 15%.

Shares are expensive at about 33x 2024 EPS because markets see this credible growth runway given how much of its pipeline is already under construction. Based on this trajectory, HLT could grow EPS by over 50% by 2027 to ~$11, absent a recession or further downturn in China. At a forward multiple of 25x reflecting its high-margin, recurring cash flow, HLT could trade towards $275 by the end of 2026.

That is about 18% upside over the next 26 months or about 8% per year. That is a market-like return, and it still assumes a fairly hefty forward multiple. In other words, Hilton shares appear to be fully discounting the significant growth the business may experience. With all of that potential good news in the price, any weakness in the economy will create downside, and it is difficult to see even greater upside catalysts.

As such, I am moving shares to a hold after Q3 results. Given mid-teens EPS growth, I do not see a need to sell or an argument to short the stock, absent a significant economic downturn. However, valuation is now full, and investors should look elsewhere for better buy opportunities.

data.title: BBB Foods Inc.: Buy The Growth
data.description:
BBB Foods Inc. (NYSE:TBBB) is a Mexican discounted grocery chain with over 2,500 stores across 15 States in Mexico. The company, BBB Foods Inc., is an acronym for "Bueno, Bonito y Barato", which translates to "Good, Nice and Affordable". In the past 5 years, the company has grown significantly and this has resulted in its share price rising by more than 50% in less than a year.

I believe this growth will only continue and improve.

The company so far has been on a strong growth trajectory. The growth story continues as the company expands its store count while maintaining high profit margins and increasing same-store sales.

BBB Foods Inc. Has Strong Historic Growth

Since 2019, the company has enjoyed same-store sales growth of over 10% annually. In its worst year during COVID in 2021, it averaged just 12.3%:

(Source: BBB Foods Inc. Financials)

Similarly, its store count has grown by ~20.2%:

(Source: BBB Foods Inc. Financials)

While its sales and stores are growing, it's keeping pace by building new distribution centers to keep its supply chain operating efficiently. BBB Foods Inc. has been building at least 1 new distribution center a year:

(Source: BBB Foods Inc. Financials)

To put things into perspective, distribution centers are a large capital outlay and companies typically do not build them unless it is to lay the groundwork for large growth.

BBB Foods Inc. Competitive Advantage

BBB Foods Inc. is a discounted dollar store and its operating strategy is focused on 4 areas:

High turnaround of its 800 products

BBB Foods Inc. has a limited selection of products and this allows the retailer to purchase a narrow selection of products in bulk at volume discounts, and favorable terms with suppliers.

Strong private label

BBB Foods Inc. owns 95 different private labels and this represents more than 400 of its products offerings. Having private labels has allowed the retailer to achieve cost savings, which are transferred to the shoppers, and the price points are much more competitive than its competition.

Outsourcing the manufacturing to a wide variety of local manufacturers

BBB Foods Inc. doesn't manufacture any of its products. Instead, it relies on a network of over 100 local suppliers to make its private label. This has kept its supply chain nimble and less reliant on one domain supplier.

Good value for money

The company's pursuit of efficiency in its supply chain and cost savings has allowed it to deliver value to consumers. Its sales have grown immensely and more than doubled since 2021 (more on this below).

IPO Has Improved BBB Foods Inc. Balance Sheet

In applying the Piotroski F-Score to analyze BBB Foods Inc. financials, its financials score a 6 to 7 in its most recent quarterly results and a score of 5 in its year ending 2023. A Piotroski F-Score of 9 shows the company's financial statements are strong while a score of 0 implies the company's financials are weak:

(Source: BBB Foods Inc. Financials)

Note, in Q1-2024 and Q2-2024, BBB Foods Inc. had not disclosed its shares outstanding, so no additional points were given on the F-Score in those periods.

Before its Feb 2024 IPO, the company was highly leveraged at 82% in 2022 and 71% in 2023. This was a concern for a company that is in high growth mode. However, its IPO in Feb. 2024 has changed that, and new equity has led to fresh cash injected into the business. Currently, its long-term debt is hovering in the mid-30%.

Its current ratio has also benefited from the IPO as its current ratio is now closer to 0.80 from 0.50 in 2022 and 2023.

In looking at its profitability, BBB Foods Inc. does have improving profitability margins. Its gross margins have improved slightly from 15% in 2021 to 17%. This percentage may seem low, but for a discounted retailer like BBB Foods Inc., it does demonstrate management's ability to find efficiencies in its supply chain.

With 2 quarters of 2024 financials now available, it does appear BBB Foods Inc. is on track to beat its 2023 net income. However, this could also be a seasonal business, so there could be fluctuations in its sales that I haven't seen yet in Q3-2024 and Q4-2024.

Summarizing all the financial figures together, I believe BBB Foods Inc. has some fixed costs in its business to overcome. Since 2019, it has doubled its warehouses across Mexico, and these are capital-intensive investments. This explains why the retailer had taken on high levels of debt prior to its IPO. To offset some of these high costs, BBB Foods Inc. has been growing its store count aggressively, and its store count has - in fact - doubled from 2019.

Also, its same-store sales growth has been growing by more than 10% each year since 2020, and with its gross margins improving slightly, this implies the company has achieved a certain level of economies of scale.

Risk: BBB Foods Inc. Is not Consistently Profitable

The downside to this buy thesis is its profit inconsistency. The company does appear to be putting growth first over profit at the moment. The path to profitability also seems certain with its improving margins. However, I do caution potential investors here because it is an element of risk. There is ample cash and leverage flexibility for BBB Foods Inc. to spend its way out of trouble, but this is not a completely safe investment.

Conclusion: BBB Foods Inc. is a Buy

Given its recent IPO early this year, I do believe not many investors are aware of this company. The past 6 months have propelled this stock price upwards, and I believe there is further roadway for it to go up.

Another reason why investors should consider this stock is it operates in Mexico so for investors who are heavily invested in North America, this is a geographic diversification play.

Mexico's GDP growth has slowed significantly. Year-over-year growth slowed to 1.0% from 1.8% the prior quarter. What this means is a lot of shoppers are probably watching their wallets closely and are likely to be price-sensitive when they grocery shop. This plays well into BBB Foods Inc.'s value proposition as a discounted supermarket.

Overall, I am bullish on BBB Foods Inc.

data.title: CARY: Strong Investment-Grade Bond ETF, Above-Average 6.2% Yield, Outstanding Risk-Adjusted Returns
data.description:
I've been discussing the Angel Oak Income ETF (NYSEARCA:CARY), a small investment-grade bond ETF, in my Investment Group throughout the year. The reason is simple: CARY has become one of my top income ETFs, due to its above-average 6.2% yield, strong performance track record, and below-average risk and volatility. Overall, risk returns are sky-high, much higher than average. CARY has a few downsides too, at least significant ones. Lots of higher-yielding CEFs and ETFs should outperform the fund long-term, but for investors looking for high-quality investments, CARY is one of the best choices out there and a buy.

CARY - Overview and Investment Thesis

Strategy and Portfolio

CARY is an actively managed ETF focusing on short-term investment-grade securities, mostly non-agency MBS and similar investments. Asset allocations are as follows:

CARY is actively managed, so asset allocations and security selection are partly active investment decisions taken by its management team. CARY chose to be overweight assorted MBS products, due to some combination of higher expected returns, income, or lower risk. Let's take a closer look at what/why this is the case, I think it's interesting, and don't want to get too repetitive in my articles. CARY's management argues that:

We believe any easing cycle will support lower interest rate volatility, which should in turn support the outperformance of mortgages and securitized credit versus corporate credit after they lagged in 2023 due to elevated interest rate volatility.

The logic behind the above is, well, complicated. Agency mortgages can be thought of as treasuries, long-term securities backed by the U.S. Federal Government, with prepayment risk, one can pay their mortgage early without penalties, unlike most bonds. Said prepayment risk can be modeled as an option (the option to pay back your mortgage without penalty). Option prices increase with volatility. In this case, investors would demand comparatively higher yields for mortgages versus treasuries, due to the possibility of significant rate cuts (would cause mortgages to be refinanced lower, treasuries would maintain their coupons). Lower interest rate volatility should lead to lower MBS rates/spreads, insofar as this is correlated with fewer cuts.

The Federal Reserve argues the same, and has a nifty graph linking mortgage spreads with rate volatility:

Although I understand the argument, can't say I agree with it, at least not meaningfully. The relationship here seems weak, indirect, and volatile. At first glance, the graph above does seem to show a relationship between spreads and volatility, but the situation wasn't so obvious as I analyzed it further. One can see how spreads have increased since mid-2022 even as volatility is flat, for instance. Wider mortgage spreads are due to the possibility of rate cuts, which can be construed as interest rate volatility, but these do seem like somewhat different phenomena.

In my opinion, MBS looks attractive because mortgage spreads are at historically above-average levels. These have rarely been higher, excepting a couple of recessions and the past year or two.

Higher mortgage spreads higher MBS spreads, a straightforward benefit for investors in these securities, including CARY. Simpler, more straightforward argument compared to interest rate volatility.

In any case, both CARY's management and I are bullish on MBS, although for different reasons.

Decisions such as outweighing MBS are meant to increase returns and income, and/or decrease risk and volatility. This specific decision has led to higher returns and outperformance, as per the fund's latest annual report. I've seen similar positions, and outperformance, in other investment-grade ETFs too.

Active funds make decisions such as the above constantly, index funds rarely do, simply blindly tracking an index (which also rarely makes decisions). Choosing an active fund means betting on the fund's management team, and on the success and effectiveness of these decisions. Index funds are simpler and somewhat less risky, but potential returns and income are both lower. I'm a big believer of active bond funds, with these having reasonable performance track records, much stronger than active equity.

As can be seen above, around half of the active bond funds in the market outperform the index, at least the cheaper ones. In my opinion, with some analysis and a small edge, one can achieve higher success rates, leading to somewhat consistent long-term outperformance. CARY definitely has, which leads me to my next point.

Outstanding Risk-Return Profile

CARY's overall risk-return profile is outstanding, with above-average income and returns, and below-average risk and volatility.

CARY's 6.2% dividend yield is higher than that of most bonds, only slightly lower than high-yield corporate bonds.

CARY's returns are quite strong too, and much more stable than average:

Both credit and rate risk are low, with the fund focusing on short-term, investment-grade securities:

Sharpe and Sortino ratios are sky-high, both since inception:

And for the past year:

Do remember that CARY's overall portfolio and strategy is somewhat conservative, with diversified high-quality holdings, focusing on MBS. There are no significant risks or negatives here, from either its overall portfolio, strategy, credit quality, rate risk, or other. Neither the fund's dividend yield nor its returns are sky-high, but both are very strong for a fund with CARY's risk and volatility.

CARY's active management is something of a risk, and could always result in some underperformance, although that has not been the case in the past. The opposite is true, with the fund outperforming because of its active management, with decisions such as overweighting commercial MBS leading to incrementally higher returns.

CARY's below-average duration should lead to lower capital gains if rates plummet. I don't think the expected set of rate cuts qualifies, nor have these led to CARY's underperformance YTD, or during the past month (closer to the most recent cut).

CARY's outperformance was mostly due to the aforementioned successful active management.

Its overall outstanding results were made possible by past Federal Reserve hikes. CARY's portfolio might have yielded 3.0% during ZIRP, with fewer opportunities for alpha (interest rate volatility caused some serious bond market dislocations). During most of the past 15 years, the only way to get +5.0% in yield was with a lot of credit risk, interest rate risk, leverage, or some combination of the three.

Federal Reserve hikes have led to higher yields across the board, with high-quality securities offering respectable income, and lots of opportunities for alpha. CARY has taken advantage of these developments and created a simple, effective, high-quality portfolio with a good yield and solid returns.

On the flip side, Federal Reserve cuts should reduce CARY's yield and return moving forward, although it will somewhat depend on the speed and magnitude of any cuts. With a duration of 3.7 years, CARY's dividends should remain competitive for several years. Long-term, dividends should decrease, dependent on future Fed policy.

Conclusion

CARY's above-average 6.2% yield, strong performance track record, and below-average risk and volatility make the fund a buy.
